title: Mindfulness meditation programs for psychological stress and well-being: a meta-analysis
citation: Modeled on Goyal M et al., 'Meditation Programs for Psychological Stress and Well-being', JAMA Internal Medicine 2014;174(3):357-368. Per-study standardized mean differences are representative test data structured to match the review's design (pooled SMD for anxiety; heterogeneous well-being evidence); they are NOT verbatim transcriptions. Grounding is evaluated against the source text in this fixture.

protocol.researchQuestion: In adults with psychological stress, do structured mindfulness meditation programs compared with active or inactive controls improve anxiety and well-being?

values.I: Structured mindfulness meditation programs (e.g., MBSR, MBCT)
values.C: Active control, usual care, or waitlist

template.name: Meditation continuous outcomes
template.instructions: Extract the reported standardized mean difference (SMD) with its 95% confidence interval for each outcome at the end of the program, plus the total number of participants analyzed.

outcomes.name: Anxiety symptoms
outcomes.description: Standardized mean difference in anxiety symptoms, meditation vs control (negative favors meditation).

outcomes.name: Psychological well-being
outcomes.description: Standardized mean difference in psychological well-being, meditation vs control.

studies.label: MBSR anxiety trial A
studies.source: Randomized trial of an 8-week mindfulness-based stress reduction program. A total of 120 adults with elevated stress were analyzed. Compared with an active control, the program reduced anxiety symptoms (standardized mean difference -0.38; 95% CI, -0.62 to -0.14). A negative value favors meditation.

studies.label: MBCT anxiety trial B
studies.source: Randomized controlled trial of mindfulness-based cognitive therapy. Among 156 participants analyzed at 8 weeks, anxiety symptoms were lower in the meditation arm (standardized mean difference -0.31; 95% CI, -0.52 to -0.10).

studies.label: Meditation anxiety trial C
studies.source: In this randomized study of 98 stressed adults, an 8-week meditation program was compared with a waitlist control. Anxiety symptoms improved with meditation (standardized mean difference -0.45; 95% CI, -0.86 to -0.04).

studies.label: Meditation anxiety trial D
studies.source: A randomized trial enrolling 140 participants evaluated a structured meditation program against an active education control. At program end, anxiety was reduced in the meditation group (standardized mean difference -0.34; 95% CI, -0.58 to -0.10).

studies.label: Well-being trial E (positive)
studies.source: Randomized trial of a mindfulness program focused on psychological well-being. Among 110 adults analyzed, the program improved well-being relative to control (standardized mean difference 0.42; 95% CI, 0.17 to 0.67), where a positive value favors meditation on this well-being scale.

studies.label: Well-being trial F (null/negative)
studies.source: Randomized controlled trial measuring psychological well-being after an 8-week meditation course. Among 132 participants analyzed, the meditation group reported lower well-being scores than the active control on the primary instrument (standardized mean difference -0.40; 95% CI, -0.64 to -0.16).
